DEBRIS.title: Orbital Debris
DEBRIS.fact: Even small debris can damage satellites because orbital speeds are extremely high.
DEBRIS.whyItMatters: More debris increases collision risk and can trigger debris cascades.

ROCKET BODY.title: Rocket Body
ROCKET BODY.fact: Spent rocket stages can remain in orbit and contribute to congestion.
ROCKET BODY.whyItMatters: Large objects are harder to avoid and may fragment into debris.

PAYLOAD.title: Satellite / Payload
PAYLOAD.fact: Some satellites become inactive and can no longer maneuver to avoid collisions.

objects.name: SOLRAD 11A/B AKM
objects.education: This rocket body helped launch SOLRAD 11, a US Navy satellite that studied solar radiation and tested anti-satellite weapons.

objects.name: OPS 3674 (VELA 4)
objects.education: VELA 4 was part of a secret program to detect nuclear explosions in space. It also discovered mysterious gamma-ray bursts!

objects.name: YZ-1 R/B
objects.education: Upper stage of a Chinese Long March rocket that launched the YZ-1 satellite. It's now space junk in a high orbit.

objects.name: ASTRON
objects.education: A Soviet ultraviolet telescope that operated from 1983 to 1989. It studied stars and galaxies in UV light.

objects.name: CXO
objects.education: Chandra X-ray Observatory – one of NASA's Great Observatories. It captures X-rays from black holes and supernovae.

objects.name: SL-12 R/B(2)
objects.education: A Soviet rocket stage from 1983 that helped launch a GLONASS navigation satellite. It's still orbiting Earth.

objects.name: TITAN 3C TRANSTAGE R/B
objects.education: This Titan rocket stage from 1969 is in a highly elliptical orbit. It's one of the oldest large debris objects.

objects.name: ARIANE 5 R/B
objects.education: Ariane 5 upper stage from 1999 that launched the XMM-Newton X-ray telescope. It's in a long, looping orbit.

objects.name: TITAN 3C TRANSTAGE R/B
objects.education: Another Titan rocket body from 1970. It's in a highly inclined orbit, slowly tumbling through space.

objects.name: XMM
objects.education: XMM-Newton is ESA's X-ray space observatory. It studies black holes, neutron stars, and galaxy clusters.

objects.name: GEOTAIL
objects.education: Joint NASA-JAXA mission to study Earth's magnetotail. It has been observing since 1992.

objects.name: TITAN 3C TRANSTAGE R/B
objects.education: Titan rocket stage from 1967. It's one of the earliest pieces of large debris still in orbit.

objects.name: YZ-1S R/B
objects.education: A Chinese rocket body from 2024. It's in a highly elliptical orbit, part of the growing debris population.

objects.name: SOLRAD 11A
objects.education: SOLRAD 11A was a US Navy satellite that monitored solar X-rays. It's still in low Earth orbit.

objects.name: TBA - TO BE ASSIGNED
objects.education: This object was recently cataloged and has not yet been publicly identified.

objects.name: TBA - TO BE ASSIGNED
objects.education: Newly detected objects are sometimes listed with placeholder names until further analysis is completed.

objects.name: PIESAT-2 09
objects.education: PIESAT-2 is a small Earth observation satellite operating in low Earth orbit.

objects.name: OBJECT F
objects.education: Objects with generic labels often represent mission-related hardware or secondary components from a launch.

objects.name: BUCCANEER MM
objects.education: BUCCANEER MM is a satellite used for technology demonstration and testing in orbit.

objects.name: COSMOS 2502
objects.education: BUCCANEER MM is a satellite used for technology demonstration and testing in orbit.

objects.name: STARLINK-1474
objects.education: Starlink satellites are part of a large constellation providing global internet coverage.

objects.name: YAOGAN-33
objects.education: Yaogan-33 is an Earth observation satellite operating in a sun-synchronous orbit.

objects.name: IMECE
objects.education: Yaogan-33 is an Earth observation satellite operating in a sun-synchronous orbit.

objects.name: OBJECT B
objects.education: Secondary objects from launches can remain in orbit and must be tracked to avoid collisions.

objects.name: YUNHAI 2-2C
objects.education: YUNHAI 2-2C is a Chinese meteorological satellite used for atmospheric research and cloud monitoring.

objects.name: GLOBALSTAR M031
objects.education: Globalstar M031 is one of the Globalstar mobile communications satellites, providing satellite phone and data services worldwide.

objects.name: SL-8 R/B
objects.education: This is a Soviet SL-8 rocket body from 1971, part of the Kosmos-3M launch vehicle. It's been orbiting for over 50 years.

objects.name: FENGYUN 1C DEB
objects.education: A piece of debris from the 2007 Chinese anti-satellite missile test that destroyed the Fengyun-1C weather satellite, creating one of the worst debris clouds in history.

objects.name: OBJECT A
objects.education: An unidentified object from a recent launch. Its purpose is unknown – could be a satellite, a rocket part, or something else.

objects.name: OBJECT F
objects.education: Another unidentified piece from a 2025 launch. Cataloged as 'OBJECT F', it's likely a deployment adapter or debris.

objects.name: PIESAT-2 12
objects.education: PIESAT-2 12 is a Chinese commercial remote sensing satellite, part of a constellation for Earth observation.

objects.name: TIANMU-1 05
objects.education: Tianmu-1 05 is a Chinese satellite for scientific research and technology demonstration.

objects.name: YAOGAN-35 C
objects.education: Yaogan-35 C is a Chinese reconnaissance satellite used for Earth observation, likely for military or civilian purposes.

objects.name: DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: This debris fragment comes from a Delta rocket launched in 1975. It's part of the growing cloud of old rocket parts orbiting Earth.

objects.name: COSMOS 1275 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from Cosmos 1275, a Soviet navigation satellite that mysteriously broke apart in 1981 – possibly due to a collision.

objects.name: COSMOS 2575
objects.education: Cosmos 2575 is a modern Russian military satellite, likely for communications or reconnaissance.

objects.name: IRIDIUM 33 DEB
objects.education: Debris from the 2009 Iridium 33 – Cosmos 2251 collision, the first major accidental hypervelocity crash between two satellites.

objects.name: OBJECT V
objects.education: A mysterious object cataloged as 'OBJECT V' from a 2022 launch – possibly a deployment adapter or mission-related hardware.

objects.name: THORAD DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: A piece of a Thorad Delta rocket from 1974. It's been in space for over 50 years, a testament to the longevity of orbital debris.

objects.name: COSMOS 2504
objects.education: Cosmos 2504 is a Russian satellite that demonstrated orbital maneuvers and debris inspection technologies.

objects.name: SL-14 R/B
objects.education: A Soviet Tsyklon-3 rocket stage from 1998 that placed a Meteor satellite into orbit. It remains in a medium Earth orbit.

objects.name: COSMOS 1054
objects.education: Cosmos 1054 was a Soviet navigation satellite from 1978, part of the Parus constellation used for submarine communications.

objects.name: COSMOS 1796
objects.education: A Soviet geodetic satellite from 1986, used to measure Earth's gravitational field.

objects.name: COSMOS 1912
objects.education: Another Soviet navigation satellite, launched in 1988. It's part of the long-lived Parus series.

objects.name: COSMOS 1092
objects.education: A Soviet early-warning satellite from 1979, designed to detect missile launches.

objects.name: GLOBALSTAR M030
objects.education: Globalstar M030 is one of the original Globalstar satellites, launched in 1999 to provide mobile communications.

objects.name: YAOGAN-30 AF
objects.education: Yaogan-30 AF is a Chinese reconnaissance satellite, part of a constellation used for electromagnetic surveillance.

objects.name: DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: Another fragment from a 1975 Delta rocket, cataloged separately. It shows how a single breakup can create hundreds of trackable pieces.

objects.name: SL-8 R/B
objects.education: A Soviet Kosmos-3M rocket body from 1985. It launched a navigation satellite and is now derelict.

objects.name: THORAD AGENA D DEB
objects.education: Debris from a Thorad Agena rocket launched in 1970. It's one of the oldest pieces of debris still tracked.

objects.name: COSMOS 827
objects.education: Cosmos 827 was a Soviet navigation satellite from 1976. It operated for several years before becoming inactive.

objects.name: YAOGAN-36 01C
objects.education: Yaogan-36 01C is a Chinese remote sensing satellite, likely used for Earth observation and reconnaissance.

objects.name: SL-8 R/B
objects.education: Another Kosmos-3M rocket body from 1981. It placed a navigation satellite into orbit and remains as debris.

objects.name: LEMUR 2 EMERSONROSE
objects.education: Lemur-2 EmersonRose is a tiny CubeSat from Spire Global, used for ship tracking and weather data.

objects.name: FENGYUN 1C DEB
objects.education: One of the thousands of fragments from the 2007 Chinese ASAT test that destroyed the Fengyun-1C satellite, creating the worst single debris cloud in history.

objects.name: SJ-21
objects.education: SJ-21 is a Chinese technology demonstration satellite that performed orbital debris capture and deorbit tests, showing how future missions could clean up space junk.

objects.name: HULIANWANG DIGUI-64
objects.education: Hulianwang Digui-64 is part of a Chinese low‑Earth orbit internet constellation, designed to provide global broadband coverage.

objects.name: VELOX 1
objects.education: Velox-1 is a Singaporean nanosatellite developed by NTU, used for technology demonstration and Earth observation experiments.

objects.name: KINEIS-4E
objects.education: Kineis-4E is a French IoT satellite from the Kineis constellation, enabling global connectivity for low‑power devices.

objects.name: AFR-1
objects.education: AFR-1 is a South African technology demonstrator, testing new satellite components and communications systems.

objects.name: COSMOS 2042
objects.education: Cosmos 2042 was a Soviet navigation satellite, part of the GLONASS system that provided positioning data during the Cold War.

objects.name: EINSTEIN PROBE
objects.education: Einstein Probe is a Chinese X‑ray astronomy satellite launched in 2024 to study black holes, neutron stars, and other high‑energy cosmic events.

objects.name: DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Delta rocket launched in 1977. It's been orbiting for nearly 50 years, a reminder of how long debris stays in space.

objects.name: SL-16 DEB
objects.education: Debris from a Zenit-2 rocket (SL-16) that launched a Russian satellite in 1993. It's one of many pieces from upper stage breakups.

objects.name: COSMOS 1473
objects.education: Cosmos 1473 was a Soviet navigation satellite from 1983, part of the Parus constellation used for submarine communication.

objects.name: FENGYUN 1C DEB
objects.education: Another fragment from the 2007 Chinese ASAT test that destroyed the Fengyun-1C satellite. This piece is part of the most debris‑dense cloud in low Earth orbit.

objects.name: COSMOS 2011
objects.education: Cosmos 2011 was a Soviet navigation satellite launched in 1989, continuing the GLONASS fleet during the late Cold War.

objects.name: CZ-4 DEB
objects.education: A piece of a Chinese Long March 4 rocket from 1999. It's a typical example of rocket body fragmentation contributing to orbital debris.

objects.name: COSMOS 2251 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from the 2009 collision between the inactive Russian satellite Cosmos 2251 and the operational Iridium 33. The crash created thousands of trackable pieces.

objects.name: YAOGAN 24
objects.education: Yaogan 24 is a Chinese remote sensing satellite, likely used for Earth observation and reconnaissance. It operates in a sun‑synchronous orbit.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0186
objects.education: OneWeb-0186 is part of the OneWeb internet constellation, designed to provide global broadband from low Earth orbit.

objects.name: COSMOS 1943
objects.education: Cosmos 1943 was a Soviet navigation satellite launched in 1988, part of the Parus constellation used for submarine communication and positioning.

objects.name: STARLINK-6278
objects.education: Starlink-6278 is one of thousands of satellites in SpaceX's Starlink megaconstellation, designed to provide global broadband internet.

objects.name: JAS 1 (FUJI 1)
objects.education: JAS-1, also known as Fuji-1, was Japan's first amateur radio satellite, launched in 1986. It enabled ham radio operators worldwide to communicate via space.

objects.name: OBJECT F
objects.education: Object F is an unidentified piece from a 2021 launch – possibly a rocket adapter or a deployment mechanism. Its exact function remains mysterious.

objects.name: CZ-6A DEB
objects.education: This fragment comes from a Chinese Long March 6A rocket that launched in 2024. It's a piece of the rocket's upper stage, now tracked as space debris.

objects.name: CZ-4 DEB
objects.education: Debris from a Chinese Long March 4 rocket launched in 1990. It's one of many fragments from decades of space missions, contributing to the orbital junk population.

objects.name: TBA - TO BE ASSIGNED
objects.education: This object is currently unassigned – it could be a newly cataloged piece of debris or a satellite awaiting identification. Its origin is a cosmic mystery.

objects.name: SL-8 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Soviet SL-8 (Kosmos-3M) rocket that launched a navigation satellite in 1991. It's a reminder of Cold War rocketry now orbiting as junk.

objects.name: STARLINK-4047
objects.education: Starlink-4047 is part of SpaceX's second-generation Starlink constellation, launched in 2022 to improve coverage and reduce latency for internet users.

objects.name: THOR ABLESTAR DEB
objects.education: This debris comes from a Thor AbleStar rocket launched in 1965. It's one of the oldest pieces of space junk still being tracked.

objects.name: FENGYUN 1C DEB
objects.education: A fragment from the 2007 Chinese anti-satellite test that destroyed the Fengyun-1C weather satellite. The event created over 3,000 trackable debris pieces.

objects.name: DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: A piece of a Delta rocket from 1978. It's part of a long‑lived debris cloud from decades ago, showing how long junk can linger in orbit.

objects.name: SL-8 R/B
objects.education: This is the upper stage of a Russian Kosmos-3M rocket that launched a military satellite in 2001. Rocket bodies like this often remain in orbit for years.

objects.name: COSMOS 1275 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from the Cosmos 1275 satellite, which mysteriously exploded in 1981 shortly after launch – possibly due to a collision or battery failure.

objects.name: COSMOS 2385
objects.education: Cosmos 2385 was a Russian military satellite launched in 2001, likely part of the Strela communications network for relaying encrypted messages.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0183
objects.education: OneWeb-0183 is part of the OneWeb internet constellation, launched in 2021 to provide broadband access to remote regions worldwide.

objects.name: QIANFAN-88
objects.education: Qianfan-88 is a Chinese satellite from the Qianfan (Thousand Sails) constellation, aimed at providing global internet coverage from low Earth orbit.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0236
objects.education: Another OneWeb satellite, launched in 2021. These satellites orbit in polar orbits to serve high‑latitude areas like Alaska and northern Europe.

objects.name: FENGYUN 1C DEB
objects.education: One of the thousands of fragments from the 2007 Chinese anti‑satellite missile test that destroyed the Fengyun‑1C weather satellite. The event created the worst single debris cloud in history, with over 3,000 trackable pieces.

objects.name: STARLINK-4380
objects.education: Starlink-4380 is part of SpaceX's second‑generation Starlink constellation, launched in 2022 to expand global broadband coverage. These satellites use ion thrusters to avoid collisions and deorbit at end of life.

objects.name: STARLINK-4388
objects.education: Another Starlink satellite from the same launch, equipped with laser inter‑satellite links to reduce latency and provide truly global connectivity without ground stations everywhere.

objects.name: ORBCOMM FM 3
objects.education: ORBCOMM FM 3 is one of the original ORBCOMM satellites launched in 1998 to provide global asset tracking and messaging services. It operates in low Earth orbit and has outlived its design life by decades.

objects.name: ARIANE 44L DEB
objects.education: A piece of debris from an Ariane 44L rocket that launched the Eutelsat II F3 and Telecom 2A satellites in 1991. It's one of many fragments from Europe's workhorse launcher, now drifting in a high‑inclination orbit.

objects.name: STARLINK-30387
objects.education: Starlink-30387 is part of SpaceX's constellation, launched in 2023. It orbits in a 43° inclination shell, helping to serve densely populated areas with low‑latency internet.

objects.name: COSMOS 1432
objects.education: Cosmos 1432 was a Soviet navigation satellite launched in 1983, part of the Parus constellation that provided position data for submarines and surface vessels. It remains in a semi‑synchronous orbit.

objects.name: NUSAT-42 (M WONENBURGER)
objects.education: NUSAT-42 is a nanosatellite named in honor of M. Wonenburger, a pioneer in mathematics. It's part of a constellation of small satellites for technology demonstration and education, launched in 2023.

objects.name: STARLINK-34125
objects.education: Starlink-34125 is a V2 Mini satellite, part of SpaceX's next‑generation design with increased bandwidth and more powerful antennas to serve remote and rural areas.

objects.name: COSMOS 1256
objects.education: Cosmos 1256 was a Soviet navigation satellite launched in 1981, part of the Parus network. It operated for many years and now silently orbits as a derelict, tracked by space surveillance radars.

objects.name: CZ-6A DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Chinese Long March 6A rocket launched in 2024. The rocket's upper stage fragmented, adding dozens of trackable pieces to the debris environment – a growing concern for space sustainability.

objects.name: FIRESAT0
objects.education: FireSat0 is a small satellite designed to detect and monitor wildfires from space. It carries infrared sensors to spot fires early, helping firefighters respond faster. Launched in 2025.

objects.name: COSMOS 1275 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from the mysterious breakup of Cosmos 1275 in 1981, just months after launch. The cause remains unknown – possibly a collision with debris or an internal explosion.

objects.name: TITAN 3C TRANSTAGE DEB
objects.education: This piece of debris comes from a Titan 3C transtage, a US Air Force rocket stage used in the 1960s and 70s. It's one of the oldest tracked objects still in orbit, a relic of the early space age.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0370
objects.education: OneWeb-0370 is part of the OneWeb constellation, launched in 2021. OneWeb aims to provide internet access to regions where fiber is impractical, especially in polar areas.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0379
objects.education: Another OneWeb satellite from the same batch, these spacecraft orbit in polar orbits at 1,200 km altitude – higher than Starlink – to cover the Arctic and northern latitudes.

objects.name: STARLINK-35020
objects.education: Starlink-35020 is a V2 Mini satellite launched in 2025, part of SpaceX's effort to increase capacity and reduce latency. It features advanced phased‑array antennas and laser links.

objects.name: COSMOS 972
objects.education: Cosmos 972 was a Soviet navigation satellite launched in 1977 as part of the Parus constellation. It provided positioning data for military and civilian use and operated for many years before becoming derelict.

objects.name: STARLINK-5121
objects.education: Starlink-5121 is part of SpaceX's Starlink constellation, launched in 2022. It operates in a 53° inclination shell and is equipped with autonomous collision avoidance systems.

objects.name: STARLINK-5592
objects.education: Starlink-5592 is a Starlink V1.5 satellite launched in 2023. It features laser inter‑satellite links to provide global coverage even over oceans and poles.

objects.name: OBJECT B
objects.education: Object B is an unidentified payload or debris from a 2025 launch. Its purpose is unknown – it could be a test satellite, a deployment adapter, or a fragment.

objects.name: GOKTURK-1A
objects.education: Object B is an unidentified payload or debris from a 2025 launch. Its purpose is unknown – it could be a test satellite, a deployment adapter, or a fragment.

objects.name: DMSP 5D-2 F11 DEB
objects.education: This debris fragment comes from a US Air Force Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP) satellite launched in 1991. DMSP satellites provide weather data for military operations.

objects.name: DELTA 2 R/B(1)
objects.education: A Delta II rocket body from a 1998 launch that deployed several Iridium satellites. Rocket bodies like this often remain in orbit for decades, contributing to debris.

objects.name: STARLINK-6261
objects.education: Starlink-6261 is part of SpaceX's Starlink Group 6-1, launched in 2023. It operates in a 43° inclination orbit to serve mid‑latitude regions with high bandwidth.

objects.name: HULIANWANG DIGUI-16
objects.education: Hulianwang Digui-16 is a Chinese satellite from the Qianfan (Thousand Sails) internet constellation, launched in 2025 to provide global broadband connectivity.

objects.name: RADIO ROSTO
objects.education: Radio Rosto was a Russian amateur radio satellite launched in 1994. It carried a transponder for ham radio operators and was part of the RS (Radio Sputnik) series.

objects.name: OBJECT B
objects.education: Object B is an unidentified piece from a 2025 launch. Its nature is unclear – it may be a satellite, a rocket stage, or debris from a breakup.

objects.name: COSMOS 2251 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from the 2009 collision between the defunct Russian satellite Cosmos 2251 and the operational Iridium 33. The crash created thousands of debris pieces, a wake‑up call for space situational awareness.

objects.name: COSMOS 2361
objects.education: Cosmos 2361 was a Russian military satellite launched in 1998, likely part of the Strela communications network. It relayed encrypted messages for Russian forces.

objects.name: METEOR 3-1
objects.education: Meteor 3-1 was a Soviet/Russian weather satellite launched in 1985, part of the Meteor series that provided global meteorological data for decades.

objects.name: CZ-4 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Chinese Long March 4 rocket launched in 1999. It's part of the debris population resulting from normal rocket stage disposal and fragmentation events.

objects.name: DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Delta rocket launched in 1975. This piece is part of a debris cloud that has been circling Earth for nearly 50 years – a testament to the longevity of orbital junk.

objects.name: SL-12 R/B(2)
objects.education: The upper stage of a Russian Proton rocket that launched a GLONASS navigation satellite in 2002. Rocket bodies like this often remain in orbit long after their mission, adding to the debris population.

objects.name: NUSAT-43 (R DIENG-KUNTZ)
objects.education: NUSAT-43 is a nanosatellite named in honor of Rose Dieng-Kuntz, a Senegalese‑French computer scientist and pioneer in artificial intelligence. It's part of a constellation for educational and technology demonstration missions.

objects.name: CZ-4 DEB
objects.education: A piece of a Chinese Long March 4 rocket launched in 1999. It's one of many fragments tracked by space surveillance networks, highlighting the challenge of tracking smaller debris.

objects.name: STARLINK-30846
objects.education: Starlink-30846 is part of SpaceX's second‑generation Starlink constellation, launched in 2023. These satellites feature laser inter‑satellite links to provide global coverage without needing ground stations everywhere.

objects.name: UME 2 (ISS-B)
objects.education: UME 2, also known as ISS‑B, was a Japanese satellite launched in 1978 to study the ionosphere and plasma bubbles. It helped scientists understand radio propagation and space weather.

objects.name: YAOGAN-30 E
objects.education: Yaogan-30 E is a Chinese reconnaissance satellite launched in 2017. It's part of a constellation believed to be used for signals intelligence and electromagnetic surveillance.

objects.name: STARLINK-33801
objects.education: Starlink-33801 is a V2 Mini satellite launched in 2025, equipped with more powerful phased‑array antennas and increased bandwidth to serve remote and rural areas.

objects.name: STARLINK-11436
objects.education: Starlink-11436 is another V2 Mini satellite from SpaceX's ever‑growing constellation. It orbits in a 43° inclination shell, helping to provide low‑latency internet across densely populated regions.

objects.name: COSMOS 2486
objects.education: Cosmos 2486 is a Russian military reconnaissance satellite, part of the Persona high-resolution optical imaging system. Launched in 2013, it provides detailed imagery for defense and intelligence purposes.

objects.name: STARLINK-31614
objects.education: Starlink-31614 is a V2 Mini satellite from SpaceX's Starlink constellation, launched in 2024. It features advanced phased-array antennas and laser links to provide high-speed internet globally, especially in remote areas.

objects.name: STARLINK-30273
objects.education: Starlink-30273 is part of SpaceX's Starlink Group 6-24, launched in 2023. These satellites orbit at 53° inclination and are designed to offer low-latency broadband to users worldwide.

objects.name: COSMOS 469
objects.education: Cosmos 469 was a Soviet military satellite launched in 1971, part of the DS-P1-Yu series used for calibration and testing of ground-based radar systems. It reentered the atmosphere after several months.

objects.name: THOR ABLESTAR DEB
objects.education: This debris fragment comes from a Thor AbleStar rocket launched in 1961. It's one of the oldest pieces of space junk still being tracked, a relic from the early days of the space age.

objects.name: SL-3 R/B
objects.education: The SL-3 rocket body is the upper stage of a Soviet Vostok-2M rocket that launched a Meteor weather satellite in 1977. It remains in orbit as a large piece of debris, slowly tumbling over decades.

objects.name: STARLINK-32241
objects.education: Starlink-32241 is a V2 Mini satellite launched in 2024, part of SpaceX's effort to provide global internet coverage. It operates in a 53° inclination shell and helps reduce latency for users in mid-latitudes.

objects.name: CSG-2
objects.education: CSG-2 (COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation 2) is an Italian Earth observation satellite launched in 2022. It uses synthetic aperture radar to monitor the planet for environmental and security applications, day or night, regardless of weather.

objects.name: TIROS 2 DEB *
objects.education: A fragment from TIROS-2, an early weather satellite launched by NASA in 1960. TIROS-2 tested experimental television cameras to observe cloud cover, paving the way for modern meteorological satellites.

objects.name: STARLINK-36585
objects.education: Starlink-36585 is a Starlink satellite launched in 2026 as part of the ongoing deployment. It's designed to provide high-speed internet to underserved areas and is part of the constellation that now numbers thousands.

objects.name: STARLINK-4471
objects.education: Starlink-4471 is a Starlink satellite launched in 2022. It operates in a 97.6° inclination sun-synchronous orbit, providing coverage to polar regions as part of SpaceX's commitment to global connectivity.

objects.name: KUIPER-00054
objects.education: Kuiper-00054 is a test satellite for Amazon's Project Kuiper, a planned constellation to provide broadband internet. Launched in 2025, it helps validate technology for the future megaconstellation.

objects.name: STARLINK-32607
objects.education: Starlink-32607 is a Starlink V2 Mini satellite launched in 2024, equipped with improved hardware to handle higher data throughput and reduce latency for users worldwide.

objects.name: NUSAT-28 (ALICE LEE)
objects.education: NUSAT-28 is a nanosatellite named after Alice Lee, a mathematician and pioneer in crystallography. Launched in 2022, it's part of a constellation of educational and technology demonstration CubeSats.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0524
objects.education: OneWeb-0524 is a satellite from the OneWeb constellation, launched in 2022. OneWeb aims to provide low-latency internet connectivity to remote and rural areas, with a focus on serving the Arctic region.

objects.name: CZ-6A DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Chinese Long March 6A rocket launched in 2024. The upper stage fragmented, creating a debris field. This piece is tracked by space surveillance networks to monitor the growing debris environment.

objects.name: STARLINK-5149
objects.education: Starlink-5149 is part of SpaceX's Starlink Group 5-1, launched in 2022. It operates in a 53° inclination orbit and helps provide low‑latency broadband internet to users worldwide.

objects.name: STARLINK-5136
objects.education: Another Starlink satellite from the same launch, these spacecraft are equipped with Hall-effect ion thrusters to raise orbit and perform collision avoidance maneuvers.

objects.name: DELTA 1 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Delta rocket launched in 1975. It's one of many pieces from decades of space activity, illustrating the long‑term problem of orbital debris.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0648
objects.education: OneWeb-0648 is part of the OneWeb internet constellation, launched in 2023. OneWeb satellites orbit in polar orbits at 1,200 km altitude, focusing on coverage of northern latitudes.

objects.name: QIANFAN-10
objects.education: Qianfan-10 belongs to China's Qianfan (Thousand Sails) megaconstellation, designed to provide global internet. Launched in 2024, it's part of a planned network of thousands of satellites.

objects.name: YUNYAO-1 46
objects.education: Yunyao-1 46 is a Chinese satellite from the Yunyao constellation, likely used for remote sensing or meteorological data collection. Launched in 2025, it contributes to Earth observation.

objects.name: CZ-4 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Chinese Long March 4 rocket launched in 1990. It's part of the debris population that has accumulated over 30 years from routine launches.

objects.name: ONEWEB-0663
objects.education: OneWeb-0663 is another satellite in the OneWeb fleet. These satellites are built on an assembly line in Florida and are designed for a five‑year operational life before deorbiting.

objects.name: SL-16 DEB
objects.education: Debris from a Zenit-2 rocket (SL-16) that launched a Russian satellite in 1998. Zenit rockets were a Soviet design, and their upper stages often fragment in orbit.

objects.name: STARLINK-11098
objects.education: Starlink-11098 is a Starlink satellite launched in 2024. It operates in a 53° inclination shell and is part of the constellation's second generation, offering higher bandwidth and reduced latency.

objects.name: SL-8 R/B
objects.education: This is the upper stage of a Russian Kosmos-3M rocket that launched a military satellite in 1992. Rocket bodies like this remain in orbit for decades, posing collision risks.

objects.name: THORAD AGENA D DEB
objects.education: A fragment from a Thorad Agena D rocket launched in 1970. The Agena upper stage was used for many US Air Force missions, and debris from its breakups still circles Earth.

objects.name: THORAD AGENA D DEB
objects.education: Another piece from the same Thorad Agena D rocket family. Over 50 years later, it remains in orbit, a silent witness to the early space age.

objects.name: STARLINK-32631
objects.education: Starlink-32631 is a V2 Mini satellite launched in 2024, featuring advanced phased‑array antennas and laser links to provide high‑speed internet globally.

objects.name: COSMOS 1275 DEB
objects.education: A fragment from the Cosmos 1275 satellite, which broke apart in 1981 under mysterious circumstances – possibly due to a collision with debris or an onboard explosion.

objects.name: SJ-11-08
objects.education: SJ-11-08 (Shijian-11-08) is a Chinese technology demonstration satellite launched in 2014. It's part of a series testing new bus designs and space environment monitoring.

objects.name: METEOR 2-20 DEB
objects.education: A piece of debris from the Meteor 2-20 weather satellite, launched in 1990. Meteor satellites were the Soviet Union's primary meteorological network, providing global weather data.
